coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent nickel based metal organic framework for enhanced sensing of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Jasjot
Kaur, Manjot
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
Umar, Ahmad
Algadi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal contamination has sparked widespread concern among the populace. The significant issues necessitate the creation of high-performance fluorescent pigments that can identify harmful elements in water. The present study deals with metal organic framework [MOF] based on nickel [Ni-BDC MOF]. The Ni-BDC MOF was prepared by facile solvothermal method using nickel nitrate hexahydrate and terephthalic acid ligand as precursors. The MOF was characterized by various techniques in order to examine the crystal, morphological, structural, composition, thermal and optical properties. The detailed characterizations revealed that the synthesized Ni-BDC MOF are well-crystalline with high purity and possessing 3D rhombohedral microcrystals with rough surface. The MOF demonstrate good luminescence performance and excellent water stability. According to the Stern Volmer plot, the tests set up under optimized conditions demonstrate a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of both ions, i.e. Fe3+, and Cr2O7 2− ions. The linear range and detection limit for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− were found to be 0–1.4 nM and 0.159 nM, and 0–1 nM and 0.120 nM, respectively. The mechanisms for the selective detection of cations and anions were also explored. The recyclability for the prepared MOF was checked up to five cycles which showed excellent stability with just a slight reduction in efficiency. The constructed sensor was also used to assess the presence of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions in actual water samples. The results of the different experiments revealed that the prepared MOF is a good material for detecting Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent nickel based metal organic framework for enhanced sensing of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal contamination has sparked widespread concern among the populace. The significant issues necessitate the creation of high-performance fluorescent pigments that can identify harmful elements in water. The present study deals with metal organic framework [MOF] based on nickel [Ni-BDC MOF]. The Ni-BDC MOF was prepared by facile solvothermal method using nickel nitrate hexahydrate and terephthalic acid ligand as precursors. The MOF was characterized by various techniques in order to examine the crystal, morphological, structural, composition, thermal and optical properties. The detailed characterizations revealed that the synthesized Ni-BDC MOF are well-crystalline with high purity and possessing 3D rhombohedral microcrystals with rough surface. The MOF demonstrate good luminescence performance and excellent water stability. According to the Stern Volmer plot, the tests set up under optimized conditions demonstrate a linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of both ions, i.e. Fe3+, and Cr2O7 2− ions. The linear range and detection limit for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− were found to be 0–1.4 nM and 0.159 nM, and 0–1 nM and 0.120 nM, respectively. The mechanisms for the selective detection of cations and anions were also explored. The recyclability for the prepared MOF was checked up to five cycles which showed excellent stability with just a slight reduction in efficiency. The constructed sensor was also used to assess the presence of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions in actual water samples. The results of the different experiments revealed that the prepared MOF is a good material for detecting Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Among all types of environmental pollution, water pollution is regarded as one of the major threats to human life and the environment. Water contamination associated with the uninterrupted release of non-biodegradable pollutants in drinking water sources emerges as a worldwide concern (Safaei et al., 2021). The long-term persistence of non-biodegradable pollutants such as organic and heavy metal contaminants in water sources necessitated the use of sophisticated detection systems for harmful metal cations and anions (Bodkhe et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2019; Li, 2020; Yildiz et al., 2017). Metal cations and anions are necessary for optimal bodily function, but only below specific allowed limitations, according to the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Wang et al., 2020). For example, Iron being indispensable element in living organisms for hemoglobin synthesis, oxygen transport and cellular metabolism, however if the permissible amount of Fe3+ present in drinking water reaches above 5.35 mm, leads to serious problems including tissue damage, diarrhoea, food aversion, skin diseases etc (Xu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019a; Tang et al., 2020; Aygün et al., 2020). Also, deficiency of iron brings about major concerns for human health including lack of sleep, immunity and is associated with diseases like Anaemia, Alzehimer and Parkinson's disease (Ma et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018; Ge et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2019). Meanwhile, the carcinogenic contaminant chromium in hexavalent form (Cr2O7 2−) poses a major hazard to human health and the environment. According to WHO, the amount of chromium permissible for drinking should be 1.0 μM (Kaur et al., 2020a; Yang et al., 2022). The widespread use of hexavalent chromium in ample number of industries namely leather tanning, steel manufacturing, refractories, pigments, agriculture etc have raised concerns for water pollution (Qiu et al., 2021). Therefore, convenient detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− is required with meticulous care.

In recent years, chromatography, ion mobility spectrometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), mass spectroscopy (MS), and other technologies have been applied for trace detection of these contaminants and pollutants (Yu et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019b; Ding et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2017; Al-Fawzan, 2020; Gu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2016). Although, these techniques carry out their task effectively but due to complexity in operation and handling of instruments, cost ineffectiveness and time-consuming process limits their applications for detection (Fan et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2021; Qi et al., 2021). Therefore, it is of paramount importance to ameliorate traditional techniques or develop other methods offering simple, cost effective and rapid detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−.

Researchers from all over the world have conducted their research work on numerous materials for the detection of heavy metal ions, including carbon dots (Singh and Kansal, 2022), graphene (Kaur et al., 2017, 2019), MOFs (Lv et al., 2019) and their composites (Kaur et al., 2022a). Although, these materials exhibit excellent sensing properties but major challenges such as sensitivity and selectivity have decelerated its usage for the heavy metal ion detection. In order to overcome these issues, a material should exhibit striking balance of sensing properties along with high sensitivity and selectivity. The growth in the discipline of porous materials has proliferated the development of metal organic frameworks. Organic linkers and inorganic secondary building units make up MOFs, which are porous crystalline frameworks (Jin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a; Chaturvedi et al., 2019).

Because of their customizable structural features, large surface area, adjustable pore size and distinctive optical properties, they have emerged as a potential value in a variety of fields, including sensing, gas storage, catalysis, water splitting and so on (Hao et al., 2021; González et al., 2021; Ali et al., 2021; Govindaraju et al., 2022; Cui and Wu, 2021; Goksu et al., 2017; Göksu et al., 2016). Particularly, luminescent MOFs employed as sensors inherent many advantages such as high sensitivity, monitoring in quick time etc (Kumar et al., 2021). Till date, some luminescent MOFs have been reported towards sensing of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions. For example, Wang et al. reported hydrothermal synthesis of cadmium microporous coordination material for fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− with detection limit 19.21 μM for Fe3+ and 12.46 μM for Cr2O7 2− (Wang et al., 2021a). Talha et al. prepared zinc-based MOF for detection of Fe3+ with the limit of detection to 2.5 μM and dichromate ions with 0.15 μM of detection limit (Talha and Li, 2022). However, the lowest detection limit for both the cations and anions is relatively rare. Thus, simple and facile method to synthesize MOF for sensing of ions like Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− still endures an appreciable challenge.

Till now, various nickel-based MOFs have attracted immense interest due to their exceptional structural and electrocatalytic capabilities (Bao et al., 2019). By controlling the type of ligands, nickel-based MOFs give us the chance to customise the environment around the Ni core. As a result, it will ensue widespread application in the areas of energy storage, heterogeneous catalysis, sensing, and biological applications (Helal et al., 2022; Sen et al., 2018). The objective of this work is predicated on the use of an accessible and affordable ligand. Terephthalic acid, the ligand used in this study, is significantly less expensive and also a bidentate ligand, which may result in a significant difference in the properties of the resulting MOFs, such as porosity and geometry. The crystal structure of Ni-BDC MOF (Fig. 1 ) includes two Ni atoms octahedrally coordinated with six O atoms. The Ni atom is coordinated by four μ3-OH and two Ocarb and the other atom of Ni is coordinated by two μ3-OH and four Ocarb (Zong et al., 2021).

In the view of foregoing considerations, the luminescence features of Ni-BDC MOF towards metal ions sensing have not been reported so far. Therefore, for the first time a sensor pioneered for the detection of both cation (Fe3+) and anion (Cr2O7 2−) using Ni-BDC MOF. A simple solvothermal method was used for the synthesis of Ni-BDC MOF which was characterized by a number of techniques in order to understand the properties and characteristics of the prepared material. Further, the Ni-BDC MOF exhibits magnificent detection for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions with anti-interference ability, outstanding reusability and lowest detection limits. The sensing mechanisms were also explored in detail. In addition, the feasibility of the prepared MOF was also analysed in real water samples for the Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions.

Without any additional purification, all of the chemical were employed as procured. Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 98.0%), ferric chloride (FeCl3), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 90%), ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), sodium azide (NaN3) were procured from brand named under S. D fine chemicals. Terephthalic acid (BDC, 99%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, ≥99.5%), and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥ 98%) were received from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99%), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, ≥97%), sodium fluoride (NaF, ≥99%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), mercury sulphate (HgSO4, ≥99%), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, ≥99%), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, ≥98.5%), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, ≥99%), ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were obtained from Merck. Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99%) and sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 98%) were acquired from Fischer Scientific. Chromium chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 98%), sodium bromide (NaBr, ≥99%), and aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O) were received from Himedia. All the experiments were conducted with double distilled water.

Ni-BDC MOF was prepared by adopting solvothermal method. Briefly, 1.0 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved into 10 ml DMF under ultrasonication for 20 min to form solution A. Meanwhile, solution B was made by dissolving 0.4 g of 1,4 benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC) entirely in 15 ml DMF while stirring. Then, progressively adding solution B to solution A, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min. After mixing, the solution was placed in an autoclave (made of Teflon-coated stainless steel) and stored in an oven at 110 °C for 24 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was kept at ambient temperature until it cooled, and the resulting green solution was washed with water and ethanol to remove any unreacted organic species before being dried at 60 °C for two days. Ni-BDC MOF powder with a green tint was obtained for characterizations and applications purposes (see Scheme 1 ).

The crystal and structural properties of as-prepared sample was done by powder x-ray diffraction (PXRD; PANanalytical X'pert Pro), using Cu-Kα radiation in the 2θ range from 5° to 90°. The functional groups of the synthesized samples were determined at room-temperature with a KBr pellet as a reference, with a range of 450–4000 cm−1 using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR; Thermoscientific Nicolet iS50) spectroscopy equipped with a diamond ATR. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; HITACHI, SU8010) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX) at a 10 kV acceleration voltage and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; TECNAI- 42200 STWIN (SEI HOLAND)) were used to analyze the structural characteristics, morphology, and elemental content of the synthesized samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI 5000 Versa Probe III model) analysis was done to understand the chemical composition of the synthesized materials. Photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000; 5J1-004 model) was used to study the optical properties of the prepared materials at room temperature.

The solutions of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions were prepared from FeCl3 and K2Cr2O7, respectively, for the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions. 1 mg Ni-BDC MOF was dissolved in double distilled water (5 ml) and ultrasonicated for 30 min to get the MOF suspension. Stock solutions of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− at the concentrations of 10−3 M were prepared. Furthermore, varied concentrations of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− were obtained by diluting a stock solution of molarity 10−3 M with a gradual dilution from 10−4 M to 10−9 M. A cuvette was filled with a 1 ml amount of MOF solution. Using a micropipette, a certain amount of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− solutions were combined with the MOF solution to obtain a combination of solution. This solution was ultrasonicated for 1 min with the excitation set at 300 nm. The associated emission levels at this excitation value were studied, and the results were then fitted to the Stern Volmer Equation to determine the lower detection limit and the Stern-Volmer constant.

Stern-Volmer equation
F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q]
where, F0 = fluorescence intensity of aqueous solution of Ni-BDC MOF, F = fluorescence intensity of Ni-BDC MOF with Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2− ions, [Q] is the concentration of quencher, i.e., Fe3+ or Cr2O7 2− ions, and KSV is Stern-Volmer constant.
Lower detection limit (LOD)

LOD for a linear curve is three times the standard deviation of the corrected calibration curve measurements divided by the slope of the curve. This can be represented by the following equation:
LOD = 3 SD/slope
Where SD is the standard deviation from the blank measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sampling procedure for real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the amount of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− present in real water, water was collected from three different sources, i.e. tap water, Lake (Sukhna Lake, Chandigarh, India), and River (Satluj River, Punjab, India). To eliminate any insoluble particles, the river and lake water samples were first centrifuged and filtered using a 0.45 mm syringe filter. The tap water sample was not treated before being utilized. Above treated water samples were spiked with various concentrations of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions solution. Under optimal conditions, the samples were ultrasonicated and additional emission values were collected using a PL spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and properties of synthesized Ni-BDC MOF
ce:section.ce:para:
The structural property, crystalline structure and phase analysis of Ni-BDC MOF were investigated using an X-ray diffraction pattern. Fig. 2a represents the XRD pattern of prepared Ni-BDC MOF. The prominent diffraction peak of synthesized Ni-BDC MOF was aligned at 2θ = 9.4°, which corresponded with the (100) plane of Ni-based material. The XRD pattern of the synthesized MOF was found to be in good agreement with the simulated data (CIF number.638,866) (Deng et al., 2018; Ahsan et al., 2019). The prepared sample had good crystallinity, as seen by the sharp and strong diffraction peaks. Besides no other impurity-related peaks were found, indicating that the sample was of excellent purity. The crystallite size was computed by using Debye-Scherrer equation
D = kλ/βcosθ
Where k represents constant, λ as X-ray wavelength, β is denoted as FWHM value and θ diffraction angle. The D is the crystallite size which was found to be 45.39 nm for the prepared MOF.

The presence of functional groups was analysed by FTIR ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 as depicted in Fig. 2b. The two strong absorption bands at 1673 and 1425 cm−1 representing COO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations for the linker BDC (Fig. S1). However, the peak for Ni-BDC MOF shifted at 1578 cm−1 indicates the symmetric stretching frequency of COO group and at 1380 cm−1 corresponds to asymmetric stretching mode of COO functional group. The separation of these above mentioned two peaks revealed that BDC carbonyl group (COO) is coordinated with Ni via a bidentate mode (Daud et al., 2022). The FTIR spectrum of the Ni-BDC MOF observed absorption peak at 3589 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibration of OH functional group. The absorption peaks at 3430 cm−1, 3350 cm−1 corresponds to presence of H2O molecule in Ni-BDC MOF. The peaks at 600-1300 cm−1 correspond to out-of-plane vibration of BDC linkers (Ahsan et al., 2020; Radhika et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021b; Mesbah et al., 2014).

The structure and morphology of Ni-BDC MOF were investigated by two techniques i.e., FESEM and HRTEM. Fig. 3 a-c represent the FESEM images of Ni-BDC MOF. 3D rhombohedral microcrystals with rough surface morphology were formed for Ni-BDC MOF that can be seen through FESEM images (Zheng et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021b). The rhombohedral microcrystals of Ni-BDC MOF were attained with average length and width ranging from 3.858 μm to 1.471 μm respectively. The TEM image shown in Fig. 3d indicates that the prepared MOF possessed the distorted rhombohedral structure with layer agglomeration. Fig. 3e shows the TEM image in which distorted rhombohedral structure with rough surfaces were observed. Whereas from Fig. 3 (f), the clear rhombohedral face of Ni-BDC MOF is evident. As a result, the imageries show good agreement between FESEM and TEM findings. Further, the composition of Ni-BDC MOF was determined by TEM-EDX spectrum as shown in Fig. S2. It was determined that the Ni-BDC MOF consists mainly of nickel, carbon and oxygen which indicates that there was no other contamination present.

Elemental mapping is extremely useful for depicting element distributions in a textural context, particularly for demonstrating compositional zonation. It was utilized to estimate the element distribution in the Ni-BDC MOF. The evenly distributed elements are seen in Fig. 4 a. Any other form of contaminant was not found in the composite, indicating the sample's excellent purity. Fig. 4b-d represents the distribution of nickel, carbon and oxygen respectively in Ni-BDC MOF.

The chemical states and surface composition of the prepared sample were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The presence of nickel, oxygen and carbon in the prepared sample was demonstrated by a survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 5 (a)), which matched the EDX results closely. The deconvoluted Ni 2p XPS spectra in Fig. 5 (b) shows two notable peaks at 856.4 eV and 874.1 eV, respectively, corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, as well as two identical satellite summits at 861.7 eV and 880 eV (Zhu et al., 2017). The two peaks in Fig. 5 (c) at 284.2 eV and 288.6 eV, which matched to the phenyl group and carboxyl group (COOH) correspondingly were noticeable for the deconvoluted C (1s) spectra (Patil and Shankarling, 2020). The peak of O (1s) was detected at 532.4 eV, which matched to O (1s) binding energy in O–C, as shown in Fig. 5 (d) (Wang et al., 2017a).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to investigate the stability of Ni-BDC MOF in a N2 environment (see Fig. 6 ). Under 170 °C, the Ni-BDC MOF thermograms reveals a weight loss of 12.4%, which is attributed to dehydration of the sample. Because of the coordination of water molecules, the second weight loss occurred about 180–230 °C. Due to the thermal degradation of the BDC ligand, the MOF was shown to be completely destroyed at temperatures ranging from 230 °C to 366 °C (Ahsan et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2016).

Because of the presence of the linker, MOFs behave as a luminous material that may be utilized to detect metal ions using luminescence sensing. At room temperature, the luminescence spectra of the Ni-BDC MOF and the ligand were analysed and they are displayed in Fig. S3. While the Ni-BDC MOF has a strong emission band at 375 nm with an excitation at 300 nm, the ligand exhibits its greatest emission peak at 364 nm. The blue shift in the emission of Ni-BDC MOF may be explained by the coordination effect between the metal ions and the ligand (Wang et al., 2022). The luminescence characteristics of Ni-BDC MOF were examined at room temperature under various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 7 a). According to the findings, increasing the excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 320 nm boosts MOF emission intensity. With a 300 nm excitation wavelength, a projecting emission peak at 375 nm was obtained and the maximum FL intensity was recorded just at 300 nm excitation wavelength. As a consequence, the excitation wavelength of 300 nm was chosen as the best for further experiments.

The luminescence behavior of Ni-BDC MOF was then examined in various solvents. Acetonitrile (MeCN), acetone, propanol, methanol (MeOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), water and ethanol were used to dissolve 1 mg of Ni-BDC MOF in a 5 ml solution of different organic solvents. As shown in Fig. 7b, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity was dictated by the type of the solvent molecules which were water > ethanol > acetonitrile > propanol > methanol > DMF > acetone. The maximum Ni-BDC emission intensity was recorded in water, owing to increased dispersion. Moreover, different solvents will shift the emission peak to varying degrees which is due to the interactions between the MOF and solvent molecules of varying polarity (Zhang et al., 2021) as shown in Fig. S4. Furthermore, the fluorescence images of Ni-BDC MOF in different solvents under UV light were shown in Fig. S5. As a result, in liquid-phase luminescence detection of ions, water may be used as an ideal dispersion solvent for Ni-BDC MOF.

In addition, the stability of Ni-BDC MOF in a high-ionicstrength environment was investigated by measuring Ni-BDC fluorescence intensities in the presence of different NaCl concentrations (0 mM–500 mM). When demonstrated in Fig. 7c, there was no discernible change in fluorescence intensity as NaCl concentrations were raised. Because the surface functional group of Ni-BDC MOF is not ionized, the observed results demonstrate that the aggregation of the Ni-BDC MOF may be regulated by changing the NaCl strength. The Ni-BDC MOF may be employed in very concentrated salt conditions due to its continuous FL intensity at higher salt concentrations (Zulfajri et al., 2019). The produced MOF's stability was also tested by measuring the PL intensity after one and two months (Fig. 7 (d)). Even after two months, there was no discernible decrease in the intensity of the emission values, demonstrating the Ni-BDC MOF's outstanding stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of cations using fluorescent Ni-BDC MOF
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, porous MOFs with Lewis basic sites such as pyridyl, amide, and hydroxyl can impact metal cations (Wu et al., 2017). To check the selectivity of prepared Ni-BDC MOF, 1 ml of 10−3 M solutions of different metal cations Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, As3+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Hg2+ were added into a 1 ml stock solution of the Ni-BDC MOF and PL spectra were recorded (Fig. 8 a). When comparing Fe3+ ions to other metal cations, significant quenching was seen, suggesting that Ni-BDC MOF might be employed as a fluorescence sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

Additionally, in the presence of various interfering ions, competitive sensing was done to assess the selectivity of the Fe3+ ion shown in Fig. 8b. The results showed that after injecting similar amounts of other cations, such as Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, As3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Hg2, the quenching impact of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of Ni-BDC remained unchanged. This suggests that the Ni-BDC MOF can be used to detect Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution selectively, even in the presence of other competing ions. Other than selectivity, another important parameter sensitivity of Ni-BDC MOF toward Fe3+ ions was also explored. The fluorescence measurements showed a declining trend as the Fe3+ concentration increased (10−3 M to 10−8 M), and it was discovered that the intensity measured at 10−8 M was the maximum, therefore it was chosen as the ideal concentration for further procedure (Fig. S6a).

As depicted in Fig. 8c, with the gradual addition of Fe3+ ions (from 0 nM to 2.6 nM) to MOF solution, decrease in FL intensity has been observed. Further, analysis provides the linear correlation (R2 = 0.98892) to fall within the range of 0–1.4 nM between the F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 8d). The limit of detection was computed from the derived formula (3σ/s, where σ and s denote the calibration plot standard deviation and slope respectively). For Fe3+ sensing, the limit was resulted as 0.159 nM. Also, the value of Stern Volmer coefficient Ksv was determined to be 1.056 × 108 M−1 which is high, implying that the fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ ions have high sensitivity and selectivity. In particular, the comparison studies of formerly reported MOF for Fe3+ sensing is described in Table 1 . The results demonstrated that the MOF can be worked as promising luminescent sensor compared to other reported fluorescent MOFs. The Ksv and detection limit was found to be comparable or superior than other MOF for Fe3+ sensing. To explore the recyclability, Ni-BDC MOF was immersed in a 10−8 M solution of Fe3+ and then ultrasonicated for 5 min to create Fe3+@Ni-BDC which was then washed many times with ethanol and water. The fluorescence intensity of the recycled MOF is remarkably close to that of the original material, as shown in Fig. 8e, suggesting that Ni-BDC may be reused for Fe3+ ion detection. After five cycles, the PL intensity of MOF did not change much, showing that Ni-BDC has good anti-interferent ability and reusability for Fe3+ detection, making it a suitable candidate for Fe3+ sensing in the real world.

As reported in the literature, there are four plausible causes for the quenching process for luminous MOFs for diverse ions (Kaur et al., 2020b, 2022b; Zhu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2022): (1) MOF structure collapse (2) targeted ion excitation or emission wavelength absorption (3) ion-exchange between the metal ions of MOFs and the targeted ions (4) The interaction of MOFs with the analyte of interest. As a result, the potential quenching mechanism for Ni-BDC MOF towards Fe3+ was investigated further. The PXRD pattern of Ni-BDC MOF following concentration with Fe3+ aqueous solution matched the XRD pattern of the synthesized sample perfectly, ruling out the possibility of (1) (Fig. S7). A wide absorption band between 280 nm and 400 nm was found in the UV visible spectra (Fig. S9) for Fe3+ ions, which encompasses the excitation peak values (300 nm) and the emission peak at 375 nm of Ni-BDC MOF.

Fe2+ ions have just one absorption band between 280 and 400 nm, which spans the range of excitation and emission values of Ni-BDC MOF but has extremely low UV absorbance. Other metal ions did not have any absorption peaks. As a result, Fe3+ ions absorb the excitation and emission light of Ni-BDC MOF, causing the luminescence intensities to decrease. Furthermore, while comparing the FTIR pattern with the produced Ni-BDC MOF, it was discovered that the interaction between Ni-BDC MOF and Fe3+ ions may be very weak because no additional absorption peaks were identified for the sample submerged in Fe3+ (Fig. S8). The recyclability of Ni-BDC MOF has resulted in weak coordination between Fe3+ ions and MOFs, since samples submerged in Fe3+ ions may be extracted using deionized water and reused for subsequent detection procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of anions using fluorescent Ni-BDC MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments on anion sensing were conducted in the same way as cation sensing experiments were performed. First to check the selectivity of prepared Ni-BDC MOF toward anions, 1 ml of stock solution of Ni-BDC MOF was added to 1 ml of 10−3 M solution of different anions that include F−, Cl−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, N3−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Cr2O7 2−, PO4 3−. The solution was transferred to the cuvette and PL spectra was recorded (Fig. 9 a). For various anions, distinct luminescence effects were seen. For example, F− anion showed almost same fluorescence intensity as shown by the solution of MOF, while the other anion shows slight change in fluorescence intensity. The most significant quenching effects were seen in dichromate ions. Only Cr2O7 2− ions displayed considerable FL quenching, which implies that the prepared Ni-BDC MOF can be utilized as FL sensors for sensitive and selective detection of Cr2O7 2− ions. Fig. 9b shows the influence of various interfering ions on the FL behavior of Ni-BDC MOF. It was found that additional interfering ions (F−, Cl−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, N3 −) to the Cr2O7 2−@Ni-BDC mixture had shown no significant effect, highlighting the exceptional selectivity of Ni-BDC for detecting Cr2O7 2−. Further, the sensitivity towards Cr2O7 2− was investigated by measuring the luminescence spectra of the MOF suspension taken at varied Cr2O7 2− concentrations (10−3, 10−4, 10−5, 10−6, 10−7 and 10−8 M). At 10−8 M concentration, it shows the highest emission intensity hence it was chosen as optimized concentration for further experiments (Fig.S6 b). The variation in luminescence quenching was observed with the addition of Cr2O7 2− ions (from 0 nM to 2.6 nM) to MOF solution as shown in Fig. 9c. The calibration curve revealed a linear relationship (R2 = 0.99131) of F0/F versus Cr2O7 2− concentration over the range of 0–1 nM in Fig. 9d. The Stern Volmer constant and the lowest detection limit was determined to be 1.680 × 108 M−1 and 0.120 nM. The Ksv and detection limit was found to be better than other reported MOF based sensor as shown in Table 2 . The recycling studies revealed that Ni-BDC MOF immersed in Cr2O7 2− anions solution regenerated easily after being washed with water (Fig. 8f). As a result, Ni-BDC MOF may be employed as a lowest detection limit fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cr2O7 2− ions in water.

PXRD pattern of Ni-BDC MOF after detection with Cr2O7 2− shows same crystallinity as MOF eliminating the possibility of collapse of the MOF framework. The FTIR spectra [Fig. S8] also shows no new absorption peaks demonstrating that quenching mechanism is not attributed to interaction between Ni-BDC MOF and Cr2O7 2. As shown in Fig. S10, the UV visible spectra of Cr2O7 2 ions covered the excitation and emission spectra of Ni-BDC MOF (300 nm and 375 nm). However, other anions show little or no absorbance. Thus, Cr2O7 2− ions absorbance in the excitation/emission range as of Ni-BDC MOF, resulted in luminescence quenching.

Tables 3 and 4
represent findingsfor Fe3+and Cr2O7 2− determination in actual water samples using the fabricated sensor, respectively. After spiking various concentrations of river water, lake water and tap water into the MOF solution, the results were found to be consistent for Fe3+and Cr2O7 2− ions that were originally added. As a result, in real-world applications, the produced Ni-BDC MOF sensor can give an ideal technique for detection of these cation and anion.

Ni-BDC MOF have been successfully fabricated by simple solvothermal technique using nickel nitrate hexahydrate and 1,4 benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC). The synthesized Ni-BDC MOF shows optical properties, water solubility and stability in high ionic environment. The XRD confirms the crystalline nature of Ni-BDC MOF. The presence of nickel, carbon and oxygen present in MOF were confirmed by XPS and EDS mapping. FESEM and HRTEM images demonstrates 3D rhombohedral microcrystal structures with rough surfaces. Under all optimized parameters, Ni-BDC MOF can be used as a sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions in an aqueous medium. It shows high selectivity toward Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was found to be 0.159 nM over the detection range of 0–1.4 nM and Ksv was found to be 1.056 × 108 M−1. While the detection limit of Cr2O7 2− was found to be 0.1 nM over the detection range of 0–1 nM and 1.680 × 108 M−1. The excellent sensitivity for both the detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions was observed with the prepared Ni-BDC MOF. The quenching mechanism of Ni-BDC MOF with Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− was mainly attributed to competitive energy absorption. Additionally, the sensor revealed good recyclability tests up to five cycles for the detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2−. Moreover, detection of Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2 ions in real water samples showed remarkable recoveries making it promising for Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− sensing in the real-world applications. Therefore, LMOF-based sensors are projected to be on the verge of practical applications with continuous work in the synthesis and optimization of novel materials.
